dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for a priority 1 report of seizures at 1656 Apt # in [CITY].
Hello, I'm Jordan, an EMT. Was 911 called for a medical emergency today?
Yes, my mother started seizing about 40 minutes ago. She's been a bit confused, too.
Thank you. Ma'am, what symptoms are you experiencing now?
Can you tell me your name and where you are?
My name is Rachel, and I'm in [CITY], I think.
When is today's date for you?
I, uh, don't really know... probably the 2nd.
Would you say you're currently seeing the nurse or a doctor in the room?
No, I think I'm home now. I live alone. I just don't feel right.
Breathing sounds normal now. I'm going to check for a strong pulse. Can I?
Sure.
Strong radial pulse and warm skin. Pulse is 144 and breathing is 6. Elevated BP-189 over 109. I'll need continuous monitoring.
Pulse confirmed at 144, strong and regular.
Respirations are at 6 per minute. Regular in depth.
Blood pressure is 189 over 109. Systolic is quite high.
SpO2 is at 89% on room air. That's low.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: Pupils are small and pinpoint. I need to use ETCO2 monitoring. May I place this tube in your nose, ma'am?

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Patient
dialogue.utterance: Okay.
Skin is warm and dry. Capillary refill time is under 2 seconds. No rashes or bruising.
Would you mind if we ask you a few more questions?
No problem.
You mentioned a seizure before. Can you tell me what happened during it?
She had two seizures-each around a minute. She was unconscious, and she wasn't aware where she was after.
Thanks. Ma'am, do you have known medical conditions?
I have epilepsy. And diabetes. I also have issues with my blood pressure.
What medications have you been using, and are you currently on any?
I used to take amlodipine, metoprolol, and some diabetes medications. I haven't taken them for two months now because I ran out.
Any current pain anywhere in your body?
No, I don't feel pain right now.
GCS is 14, eye 4, verbal 4, motor 6. Alert, but disoriented.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia at a high rate.
Blood glucose is at 244. Discussing possible diabetes management.
We need to put in an IV line. This may help us administer medications. May I start one on your arm or leg?
Of course.
IV start attempted in both arms unsuccessful. Switching to EJ.
I'm going to use your external jugular vein. This may pinch a bit, but it's preferred for faster access.
IV Start in EJ successful. 0.5 mg Narcan administered. Watch for improvement.
Pulse decreased to 132 after medication. Still elevated, but slower.
Breathing improved to 15 per minute and steady.
Blood pressure is now 212 over 182. Still high.
SpO2 improved to 99%. We're trending in the right direction.
Placing 4 lead ECG per protocol. It should give us more detail on cardiac status.
Narcan had a positive effect. We will continue monitoring as we prepare for transport.
Pulse dropped to 110, respiratory rate 17. Blood pressure 200 over 99. No significant deterioration.
SpO2 still at 98% on continuous monitoring.
IV site in external jugular holds stable. No signs of infiltration or swelling.
Glucose still reads 0. This finding isn't reliable. Likely due to sensor limitations or medication interference, will be rechecked at the hospital.
Initiating medical - altered mental status (protocol 3 - 15).
Do you have a preference for the hospital you'd like to go to?
She should go to [COMPANY_NAME]. It's the closest and we trust their care there.
We're going to move you onto the stretcher now. Partner, ready the 5-point harness.
She is secured. Monitoring vitals as we mobilize.
Respiratory rate is 17. Improved from 6. Strong and steady breathing.
Patient is a 64-year-old woman with a past medical history of epilepsy, hypertension, and non-insulin-dependent diabetes, plus a history of opioid abuse.
She has not taken any of her medications for approximately 2 months and was witnessed having two seizures today.
Her initial vitals were 189/109, 144, resp 6. After Narcan, vitals are better: 200/99, 110, resp 17.
Narcan was administered via external jugular IV at 0.5 mg, and she showed quick and positive response to treatment.
Vital signs are improving with IV access in place. EKG shows sinus tachycardia. Oxygen saturation has improved to 98%.
All monitoring devices in place: EKG, pulse ox, ETCO2, and glucose meters. All systems are online and functioning.
We will turn her over to the hospital staff once we arrive.
Approaching [COMPANY_NAME] now. Calling ahead as requested to prepare for her arrival.
Delivering patient to Bed 24 as arranged. Handover process is continuing now.
